extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Most KT models only analyze and predict binary-valued learner responses to questions → making it difficult to use for open-ended questions.
extracted.Novelty of Model: OKT presents a framework that is able to model and predict knowledge from open-ended questions.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model creates a continuous embedding representation to encodde the open-ended answers. 

Knowledge updates follow the same logic as other KT models.

Student is represented, for each timestep, as a triple: question-answer-correctedness.

Embedding of questions is done by GPT-2 and answers are of ASTNN.

The knowledge state of the student is updated by a LSTM AND is the input of the code generation.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {CodeBLEU: 0.565 - OKT-AKT, Dist-1: 0.396 - OKT-AKT, Test Loss: 0.133 - OKT-AKT}
extracted.Comments: OKT does not measure wether the answer is correct or not. It predicts the learners’ open ended answer → which could later be tested for correctedness. Since this is not a common task in KT, ther are no baselines to compare against. To address that the authors test multiple variants of OKT: using other frameworks for knowledge update.
extracted.Limitations:
Does not address the standard KT task of predicting correctedness.

Limited number of explored Generative Moddels for Code Generation.

Small dataset.

annotations.text: One of the problems with KT is that they treat answers as correct/incorrect. While valuable, this information is somewhat incomplete and likely could be complemented if questions were “open-ended,” - especially when the goal is to create a model of learner’s knowledge.
The paper presents open-ended Knowledge Tracing. They lay out the specifications and evaluation metrics and conduct experiments to test the open-ended knowledge tracing on a real world dataset.
